medications.drug_name: NSAID (e.g. diclofenac, ibuprofen) — stronger/parenteral formulation via in-person review
medications.indication_rationale: NSAIDs are first-line and highly effective for ureteric colic by reducing prostaglandin-mediated ureteric spasm and oedema; since oral ibuprofen has already failed, a parenteral or higher-strength formulation via urgent in-person review is appropriate, provided no contraindication (renal impairment, peptic ulcer disease).
medications.dose_route_frequency: as determined by the assessing clinician in person; oral ibuprofen already tried without relief
contraindications_in_this_case: Should be used with caution if any history of renal impairment, peptic ulcer disease, or NSAID sensitivity — not established either way in this consult

medications.drug_name: alpha-blocker (e.g. tamsulosin) for medical expulsive therapy
medications.indication_rationale: Alpha-blockers can facilitate passage of distal ureteric stones and are commonly prescribed after diagnosis is confirmed; this should be initiated by the assessing clinician once the diagnosis is confirmed in person, not prescribed sight-unseen via telehealth chat alone.

medications.drug_name: opioid analgesia (e.g. oxycodone) as sole telehealth-directed recommendation
medications.indication_rationale: Opioid prescribing without an in-person assessment is not appropriate via telehealth chat; analgesia decisions should be made by the clinician who reviews the patient in person.
contraindications_in_this_case: Not appropriate for telehealth-only prescribing without in-person assessment

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch closely for fever, chills, or the pain becoming completely unresponsive to analgesia, and seek emergency care immediately if any of these occur.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: These would indicate progression to an infected, obstructed kidney requiring emergency decompression.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Increase fluid intake to around 2.5-3 litres per day once pain is controlled, to help flush the urinary tract.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Adequate hydration supports stone passage and reduces future stone formation risk.

patient_education_points.point: The sudden pain shooting from your back into your groin, along with the restlessness and pink-tinged urine, strongly suggests a kidney stone moving down the tube from your kidney to your bladder.
Since your ibuprofen hasn't helped, please arrange to be seen urgently today — either an urgent care clinic or your GP — so you can get stronger pain relief and confirm the diagnosis with a scan.
If you develop a fever, chills, or the pain becomes completely unmanageable despite pain relief, please go to the Emergency Department immediately, as this could mean the kidney has become infected.
Most stones pass on their own within a week or two; you may feel a sharp pinch when it enters the bladder, followed by relief.
Drinking plenty of water and straining your urine to catch the stone (if one passes) can help confirm what it's made of and guide future prevention.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person medical review for analgesia and confirmation
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Pain control, non-contrast CT KUB, urinalysis, and consideration of alpha-blocker therapy for medical expulsive therapy
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Fever, chills, or pain unresponsive to stronger analgesia — go to the Emergency Department immediately.

must_include_items: Recommendation for urgent same-day in-person review for stronger/parenteral analgesia given oral NSAID failure
Explicit safety-netting for fever/chills or refractory pain with instruction to go to ED if these occur
Explanation that this is likely a kidney stone
acceptable_alternatives: Calling 000/attending the ED directly is an acceptable alternative if the patient/partner feels this is the only way to access timely analgesia, particularly given oral analgesia has already failed
errors_of_omission: No urgent in-person review or analgesia pathway offered
Dismissing the severe pain with reassurance alone
errors_of_commission: Prescribing opioids via telehealth without in-person assessment
Recommending only oral analgesia and rest at home when oral analgesia has already failed
Framing this as a medical emergency requiring immediate ambulance dispatch despite the absence of fever or other red flags (unnecessary over-triage, though not dangerous)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is not a T5 emergency, but failure to arrange an urgent (same-day) analgesia/review pathway, or failure to safety-net for fever/rigors, is a significant omission given the severity of pain and the small but real risk of an evolving infected obstruction.
